title: Working Tree, Staging Area, and History
summary: Follow one file from an ordinary edit through the staging area into a committed snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: The Working Tree Explained
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the directory where you make changes to your files. It's the first area where your files reside when you start working on a project. Any modifications you make to files in this directory are considered untracked by Git until you explicitly stage them. 

Worked example: Imagine you have a file named `report.txt` in your project directory. You open it and add some new content. At this point, the changes are only in the working tree. You can use `git status` to see that `report.txt` is modified but not yet staged.

sketchBlocks.title: What is the Staging Area?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area, also known as the index, is a place where you can group changes before committing them. It acts as a buffer between the working tree and the commit history. You add changes to the staging area using the `git add` command.

Worked example: Continuing with `report.txt`, after editing, you decide to stage the changes. You run `git add report.txt`. Now, `report.txt` is in the staging area, ready to be committed. You can verify this by running `git status` again, which will show `report.txt` as staged for commit.

sketchBlocks.title: From Staging to Commit History
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Committing is the process of saving your staged changes to the repository's history. Each commit is a snapshot of your project at a point in time. You use the `git commit` command to create a commit.

Worked example: With `report.txt` staged, you commit the changes using `git commit -m "Update report with new data"`. This command creates a new commit in the history, capturing the current state of `report.txt`. You can view the commit history with `git log --oneline`.

sketchBlocks.title: Git Status as a Bridge
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
`git status` is a crucial command that helps you understand the state of your working tree and staging area. It shows which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git.

Worked example: After making changes to multiple files, running `git status` provides a summary of your current repository state. It tells you which files are modified, which are staged, and which are untracked, helping you decide your next steps.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of Version Control
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
options: To track changes and collaborate on projects
To edit files directly on the server
To replace the need for backups
quizDraft.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes.

help.concept: Version control systems track changes to files over time.
help.hint_1: Version control helps manage changes and collaboration.
help.hint_2: It allows you to revert to previous versions of files.

quizDraft.title: Understanding the Staging Area
quizDraft.prompt: What role does the staging area play in Git?
options: It holds changes before they are committed
It is where files are edited
It is used to delete files
quizDraft.hint: Consider what happens to files in the staging area.

help.concept: The staging area is where changes are prepared for commit.
help.hint_1: The staging area holds changes before they are committed.
help.hint_2: It acts as a buffer between the working tree and commit history.

quizDraft.title: Components of a Git Repository
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.title: Order of Git Operations
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the following Git operations in the correct order.
tokens: Edit files in the working tree
correctOrder: Edit files in the working tree
quizDraft.hint: Consider the typical workflow from editing to committing changes.

help.concept: The typical Git workflow involves editing, staging, and then committing changes.
help.hint_1: Start with editing files in the working tree.

quizDraft.title: Track Changes in the Working Tree
quizDraft.prompt: You have a file `notes.txt` in your working directory. Edit the file to add a new line of text, then check the status of your working tree using Git commands. Type the command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Edit the file and use a Git command to check its status.

help.concept: The working tree is where you make changes to files before staging them.
help.hint_1: Use a text editor to modify `notes.txt`.
help.hint_2: Run `git status` to see the changes in the working tree.

quizDraft.title: Stage Changes for Commit
quizDraft.prompt: You have modified `notes.txt` in your working directory. Stage this file for commit using the appropriate Git command. Type the command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use a Git command to stage the file.

help.concept: The staging area is where you prepare changes for a commit.
help.hint_2: Run `git add notes.txt` to stage the changes.
